mapping.a_element: MACE (a finite model builder that translates first-order statements to propositional clauses and uses a Davis-Putnam-Loveland-Logeman procedure to find models)
mapping.b_element: Mace4 (a finite model builder used to produce distinguishing examples for groupoid identities)

mapping.a_element: Otter (a theorem prover searching for proofs of first-order statements)
mapping.b_element: Otter (an automated theorem prover used to produce proofs for equational bases, e.g., in Boolean algebras)

mapping.a_element: search for finite models (countermodels) of first-order statements
mapping.b_element: search for finite models (distinguishing examples) of groupoid identities

brief_justification: While both papers mention the use of MACE/Mace4 and Otter as computational tools, their core mechanisms differ: Paper A focuses on the general method of finite model finding for first-order logic via propositional satisfiability, whereas Paper B uses finite model building as a tool to explore algebraic properties of groupoids and equational identities. The underlying machinery (propositional SAT solving vs. algebraic identity classification) is distinct.
